jokes.text: it was made of salami.
jokes.setup: Historical setup about Galileo's telescope being made in 1609
jokes.punchline: Revealing it was made of processed meat instead of glass/metal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist misdirection that subverts historical expectation with deli meat. Perfect economy and surprising logic.

dialogue.line: it was made of salami.

jokes.text: Hey... buona notte. That's-a nice suit.
jokes.setup: Supposed to be playing Galileo in historical sketch
jokes.punchline: Breaks into stereotypical Italian-American accent with modern compliment
jokes.explanation: Mild character break moment, relies somewhat on ethnic stereotype but fits the sketch's absurdist tone.

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: Hey... buona notte. That's-a nice suit.

jokes.text: Well, I can see the morta della constellation.
jokes.setup: Looking through salami telescope at the sky
jokes.punchline: Mortadella constellation - Italian deli meat pun
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that maintains the salami conceit with astronomical terms.

dialogue.line: Well, I can see the morta della constellation.

jokes.text: And the planet bologna.
jokes.setup: Continuing the celestial observations
jokes.punchline: Bologna as planet name
jokes.explanation: Solid continuation of the meat/space wordplay pattern, though less surprising than the first.

dialogue.line: And the planet bologna.

jokes.text: Oh, and there goes the entire 'piccoloaf' galaxy.
jokes.setup: Final celestial observation in the sequence
jokes.punchline: Piccoloaf - combining piccolo with meatloaf
jokes.explanation: Most clever of the wordplay sequence - piccoloaf is genuinely inventive portmanteau.

dialogue.line: Oh, and there goes the entire 'piccoloaf' galaxy.

jokes.text: Mmm... God, this salami stinks.
jokes.setup: Ad-libbed reaction breaking character during sketch
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging the prop's actual smell problem
jokes.explanation: Natural reaction that breaks the sketch's reality but creates authentic behind-the-scenes comedy.

dialogue.line: Mmm... God, this salami stinks.

jokes.text: Well, that's 'cause you're looking through the wrong end.
jokes.setup: Partner responding to complaint about smell
jokes.punchline: Logical telescope advice applied to smelly salami
jokes.explanation: Perfect application of telescope logic to the salami situation - economical and logical.

dialogue.line: Well, that's 'cause you're looking through the wrong end.

jokes.text: Evidently, Bob barker has no remaining sense of smell.
jokes.setup: Explaining the 10-year-old salami came from Price is Right set
jokes.punchline: Deducing Bob Barker's olfactory limitations
jokes.explanation: Sharp industry joke with logical progression - very Artie-like cynical observation.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Evidently, Bob barker has no remaining sense of smell.

jokes.text: No. It's very difficult to find someone who can make a salami telescope that looks functional and that doesn't stink.
jokes.setup: Asked about finding new propmaster
jokes.punchline: Treating salami telescope as legitimate specialized skill
jokes.explanation: Artie's deadpan treatment of absurd prop requirements as serious professional challenge is perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: No. It's very difficult to find someone who can make a salami telescope that looks functional and that doesn't stink.

jokes.text: Artie, I don't smell, do I?
jokes.setup: After discussion of stinky salami and smells
jokes.punchline: Larry's neurotic self-doubt about his own odor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders neurosis - turning prop discussion into personal insecurity.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Artie, I don't smell, do I?

jokes.text: 9:00? That's too late. I can change it to 8:00. Ahem. 8:30? Well... 8:30. 9:00 sounds right. All right.
jokes.setup: Beverly trying to accommodate Larry's dinner reservation preference
jokes.punchline: Larry's indecisive neuroses lead them full circle back to original time
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showcasing Larry's indecisiveness. Good escalation structure with circular logic.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: 9:00? That's too late. I can change it to 8:00.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Ahem. 8:30?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: 9:00 sounds right. All right.

jokes.text: They have an umbrella cockatoo that already says, 'hello, daddy.'
jokes.setup: Larry looking for a bird
jokes.punchline: Pre-trained bird with suggestive greeting
jokes.explanation: Mildly suggestive bird dialogue that fits Larry's character needs.

dialogue.line: They have an umbrella cockatoo that already says, 'hello, daddy.'

jokes.text: Tasmanian two tufted canary has a lovely bel canto. ♪br-r-r-r-r ♪ ♪r-r-roo-roo ♪
jokes.setup: Beverly suggesting songbird instead
jokes.punchline: Artie's operatic bird impression with made-up breed name
jokes.explanation: Artie's unexpected musical performance with fake expertise is quintessential character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Tasmanian two tufted canary has a lovely bel canto. ♪br-r-r-r-r ♪ ♪r-r-roo-roo ♪

jokes.text: I honestly don't want to see Leno get one fucking new viewer just because they disagree with my stand on affirmative action and don't know his opinion about anything.
jokes.setup: Beverly asking Larry to sign affirmative action petition
jokes.punchline: Larry's neurotic competitive concern about Leno gaining viewers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders neurosis - turning social issue into personal ratings anxiety with sharp industry insight.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I honestly don't want to see Leno get one fucking new viewer just because they disagree with my stand on affirmative action and don't know his opinion about anything.

jokes.text: Um, do you want Mexican food or Japanese food tonight?
jokes.setup: Clyde confronting Beverly about racism on the show
jokes.punchline: Beverly's desperate subject change to avoid the conversation
jokes.explanation: Realistic awkward deflection that shows character's discomfort with confronting difficult topics.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Um, do you want Mexican food or Japanese food tonight?

jokes.text: This show is like the waltons. They got their little colored girl takin' out the garbage.
jokes.setup: Beverly defending the show isn't racist
jokes.punchline: Clyde's harsh comparison to wholesome family show with racial dynamic
jokes.explanation: Sharp, uncomfortable comparison that uses familiar TV reference to make serious point about workplace dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Clyde
dialogue.line: This show is like the waltons. They got their little colored girl takin' out the garbage.

jokes.text: You wanna be the only fly in the buttermilk?
jokes.setup: Continuing his critique of Beverly's situation
jokes.punchline: Vivid metaphor for being token minority
jokes.explanation: Colorful, memorable metaphor that crystallizes the tokenism critique effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Clyde
dialogue.line: You wanna be the only fly in the buttermilk?

jokes.text: Too much affirmative talk and not enough action.
jokes.setup: Criticizing Larry's liberal lip service
jokes.punchline: Playing on 'affirmative action' phrase structure
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that makes political point while deconstructing the phrase itself.

dialogue.speaker: Clyde
dialogue.line: Too much affirmative talk and not enough action.

jokes.text: He's gonna be askin' you to shine his shoes.
jokes.setup: Predicting Larry will ask Beverly to do more demeaning tasks
jokes.punchline: Specific reference to historical racial servitude
jokes.explanation: Predictable but effective escalation that makes the tokenism critique more explicit.

dialogue.speaker: Clyde
dialogue.line: He's gonna be askin' you to shine his shoes.

jokes.text: Say, beverly, listen, you know those shoes the wardrobe department sent over? ...would you mind lacin' them up?
jokes.setup: Clyde just predicted Larry would ask Beverly to handle his shoes
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately asking Beverly about his shoes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dramatic irony - Larry unknowingly fulfills Clyde's prediction immediately, creating devastating comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Say, beverly, listen, you know those shoes the wardrobe department sent over? ...would you mind lacin' them up?

jokes.text: Close enough?
jokes.setup: Larry's shoe request perfectly confirming Clyde's prediction
jokes.punchline: Clyde's understated 'I told you so' moment
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic punctuation - economical, understated, and dramatically satisfying payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Clyde
dialogue.line: Close enough?

jokes.text: His skirt was at the dry cleaners, so there was a conflict. He couldn't do it.
jokes.setup: Larry asking why Wesley Snipes wasn't on after Patrick Swayze in drag
jokes.punchline: Absurd literal explanation for booking conflict
jokes.explanation: Paula's dry absurdist logic applied to booking decisions - typical of her character.

dialogue.line: His skirt was at the dry cleaners, so there was a conflict. He couldn't do it.

jokes.text: Because black people find Larry to be white.
jokes.setup: Larry asking why they don't have more black guests
jokes.punchline: Paula's matter-of-fact assessment of Larry's whiteness
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly simple and devastating - cuts right to the core issue with perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Because black people find Larry to be white.

jokes.text: You want me to turn gay, don't you? Oh... yes, please. Make all of San Francisco's dreams come true.
jokes.setup: Hank asking if he's attractive and getting positive response
jokes.punchline: Paula's enthusiastic encouragement for him to be gay
jokes.explanation: Paula's deadpan enthusiasm about Hank's potential gayness with geographic specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You want me to turn gay, don't you?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Oh... yes, please. Make all of San Francisco's dreams come true.

jokes.text: Oh, well, Larry won't hire them. I mean, personally, I think it's terrible. I think it's unfortunate and embarrassing for all of us, but, as Larry said, in the super-secret memo, 'if it ain't white, it ain't right.'
jokes.setup: Beverly asking why no black writers on the show
jokes.punchline: Phil's fake racist memo attribution to Larry
jokes.explanation: Phil's characteristic troublemaking with fake inflammatory quote - perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Oh, well, Larry won't hire them. I mean, personally, I think it's terrible. I think it's unfortunate and embarrassing for all of us, but, as Larry said, in the super-secret memo, 'if it ain't white, it ain't right.'

jokes.text: You are such an asshole, Phil, you know that? Yeah. You know, if you're writing an article, don't use my name.
jokes.setup: Beverly calling out Phil for his fake racist memo claim
jokes.punchline: Phil's casual acceptance and media concern
jokes.explanation: Phil's shameless acceptance of being an asshole with practical media concerns.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: You are such an asshole, Phil, you know that?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: You know, if you're writing an article, don't use my name.

jokes.text: It's just Larry hogs all the freebies.
jokes.setup: Explaining why she's asking Victoria Principal about hair products
jokes.punchline: Casual complaint about Larry taking all the guest gifts
jokes.explanation: Realistic behind-the-scenes complaint about host privilege with products.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: It's just Larry hogs all the freebies.

jokes.text: She's in love with wesley snipes. Well, he's really cute, but I... excuse me.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Wesley Snipes booking
jokes.punchline: Beverly's awkward retreat from the conversation
jokes.explanation: Mild workplace awkwardness about celebrity attraction with racial undertones.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: She's in love with wesley snipes.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, he's really cute, but I... excuse me.

jokes.text: People always say that, but, you know, when I put the stuff on my face, I break out. She can get her money back.
jokes.setup: Victoria Principal saying her products work for all races
jokes.punchline: Paula's dismissive customer service response
jokes.explanation: Paula's practical dismissal of Beverly's skincare concerns shows workplace dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: People always say that, but, you know, when I put the stuff on my face, I break out.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: She can get her money back.

jokes.text: It's a fruit phone. Put the banana up to your ear.
jokes.setup: Someone presenting a novelty phone concept
jokes.punchline: Literal fruit phone using banana as handset
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag with fruit-based technology - mildly amusing prop comedy.

dialogue.line: It's a fruit phone. Put the banana up to your ear.

jokes.text: Well... if you mean can he make a salami telescope, yeah, I think so.
jokes.setup: Larry asking if Clyde has props experience
jokes.punchline: Beverly referencing the specific ridiculous prop from earlier
jokes.explanation: Good callback that brings the absurd prop back into the workplace discussion.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well... if you mean can he make a salami telescope, yeah, I think so.

jokes.text: Black people will never have as much experience as white people because up till 10 years ago, black people had no props.
jokes.setup: Larry saying they need someone with experience
jokes.punchline: Beverly's pointed observation about historical exclusion
jokes.explanation: Sharp political observation disguised as TV industry history - cuts to core of systemic exclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Black people will never have as much experience as white people because up till 10 years ago, black people had no props.

jokes.text: I would like some yogurt.
jokes.setup: Beverly making passionate case for hiring Clyde
jokes.punchline: Larry's complete subject change to avoid the conversation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders avoidance behavior - deflecting serious conversation with mundane need.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I would like some yogurt.

jokes.text: Baby, you're talkin' like it's Friday, and it's only Wednesday.
jokes.setup: Beverly saying she can't work for Larry anymore
jokes.punchline: Paula's way of saying Beverly is having a midweek breakdown
jokes.explanation: Clever way to describe someone having a premature emotional breakdown - very Paula.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Baby, you're talkin' like it's Friday, and it's only Wednesday.

jokes.text: Yeah. Well, if Larry asks, tell him I went out to get his yogurt.
jokes.setup: Beverly leaving for job interview at Arsenio
jokes.punchline: Uses Larry's yogurt request as cover story
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Beverly using Larry's selfishness as cover for rebellion.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Yeah. Well, if Larry asks, tell him I went out to get his yogurt.

jokes.text: Forget our confusion. You know, we always had the impression that Hank was an only child.
jokes.setup: Hank introducing his 'sister' Wendy
jokes.punchline: Larry's polite way of calling out Hank's obvious lie
jokes.explanation: Larry's diplomatic skepticism about Hank's obvious fabrication - well-crafted character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Forget our confusion. You know, we always had the impression that Hank was an only child.

jokes.text: It's all connections. That's how your... your son got the job. Good-looking boy.
jokes.setup: Hank arguing business is about connections
jokes.punchline: Hank's awkward compliment about Artie's son
jokes.explanation: Hank's inappropriate personal comment while making business argument - typical awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It's all connections. That's how your... your son got the job. Good-looking boy.

jokes.text: Hello. Larry Sanders show. Who's calling? Larry's in a meeting at the moment. Could you please hold? Thank you and... cheerio.
jokes.setup: Wendy demonstrating her phone skills for the assistant job

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Hello. Larry Sanders show. Who's calling? Larry's in a meeting at the moment. Could you please hold? Thank you and... cheerio.

jokes.text: I'm having a stroke.
jokes.setup: Watching Wendy's terrible phone demonstration
jokes.punchline: Artie's medical hyperbole for his reaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie reaction - understated yet dramatic response to incompetence.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'm having a stroke.

jokes.text: Well, I see that talent runs like a cancer through the Kingsley household.
jokes.setup: Observing both Hank and Wendy's incompetence
jokes.punchline: Comparing family talent to cancer spread
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark metaphor that perfectly captures Artie's wit - 'talent runs like cancer' is genuinely clever wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, I see that talent runs like a cancer through the Kingsley household.

jokes.text: otherwise elly may will be cutting up your fruit and telling you how loverly the show was last night.

jokes.punchline: Beverly Hillbillies reference with phonetic accent
jokes.explanation: Artie's cultural reference comparing Wendy to hillbilly TV character - effective put-down.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: otherwise elly may will be cutting up your fruit and telling you how loverly the show was last night.

jokes.explanation: Larry's absurd testimonial about using cooking utensils for shaving - good talk show parody.

dialogue.speaker: Larry

jokes.text: if it's a weeknight.
jokes.setup: Victoria asking if he does this routine on his face

jokes.explanation: Weird personal detail that suggests Larry has different grooming standards for different nights.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: if it's a weeknight.

jokes.setup: Victoria Principal being very wealthy from her products
jokes.punchline: Larry's absurd image of her literally using money for skincare
jokes.explanation: Vivid absurd image of wealth-based beauty routine - good talk show host banter.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: you must just go home and rub cash on your face.

jokes.setup: Larry and Beverly having intense personal conversation on-air

jokes.explanation: Guest's confusion about the inappropriate personal discussion happening during her interview.

dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Victoria

dialogue.line: No.

jokes.setup: Wendy describing meeting Larry as exciting as meeting Jack Nicholson
jokes.punchline: Her physical reaction being mistaken for geological event
jokes.explanation: Wendy's over-the-top celebrity reaction story with earthquake comparison - moderately amusing.

dialogue.line: It's like when I met Jack nicholson, I died. I mean, I started kicking everyone all around. People thought it was some sort of an earth tremor or something.

jokes.explanation: Disturbing craft detail that makes the already weird night light even more unsettling.

dialogue.line: It's an elizabeth Taylor night light, and she just whipped it up in 15 seconds. ...And, by the way, this is actual hair.

jokes.setup: Examining the Elizabeth Taylor night light with real hair
jokes.punchline: Practical safety concern about Larry's flammability
jokes.explanation: Artie's practical assessment that escalates from aesthetic to safety concerns - good character logic.

dialogue.line: Listen, not only is it ugly, it's a fire hazard. If Larry turned this on, he'd go up in flames.

jokes.text: $800 a week. Stick with Larry.

jokes.punchline: Father's immediate practical response
jokes.explanation: Father's pragmatic advice cutting through Beverly's emotional conflict - good economy.

jokes.explanation: Inappropriate workplace sentiment that crosses professional boundaries - creates good awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly

dialogue.speaker: Beverly

jokes.text: That's why we haven't had him on. You're fun.

jokes.text: Oh, hell, cut the crap. That's not your sister. I saw you dry-humping under the bleachers.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Fucked by the black man again?

dialogue.line: Did you ever think would heavy d would let hisself get that fat? I don't think I could let myself get that fat.
